coredata.dc:title: In-situ NO release and conversion for highly efficient synergistic gas therapy and phototherapy

coredata.dc:description: In this paper, simple nitrated carbon dots (CDs-NO) were prepared as NO donor as well as photosensitizer to integrate gas therapy and phototherapy therapy into one nanoparticle. Firstly, carbon nanodots (CDs) were synthesized by using methylene blue (MB) as carbon source via hydrothermal strategy with H2SO4 as oxidization and dehydration (carbonization) agent. On account of the aromas property and the present heteroatom of MB, as-synthesized CDs possess great NIR-II harvest and photothermal conversion ability (29.7 %). To take advantage of the abundant incoming –OH, nitration treatment is performed to obtain CDs-NO, which produces NO gas under the stimuli of glutathione (GSH) and hyperpyrexia. The consumption of GSH also benefits to the elevation of intracellular oxidative stress. Under NIR-II (1064 nm) irradiation, the generated ROS would combine with NO in-situ to form ONOO− radicals, which possess the higher cytotoxicity to cancer cells. The short distance between ROS and NO makes sure the quick conversion and high utilization of NO. The synergistic NO therapy and photo-thermal/dynamic therapy not only reveals remarkable therapeutic effect for tumor bearing mice but also arouses immune response for metastasis inhibition efficiently.

head.ce:title: In-situ NO release and conversion for highly efficient synergistic gas therapy and phototherapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, simple nitrated carbon dots (CDs-NO) were prepared as NO donor as well as photosensitizer to integrate gas therapy and phototherapy therapy into one nanoparticle. Firstly, carbon nanodots (CDs) were synthesized by using methylene blue (MB) as carbon source via hydrothermal strategy with H2SO4 as oxidization and dehydration (carbonization) agent. On account of the aromas property and the present heteroatom of MB, as-synthesized CDs possess great NIR-II harvest and photothermal conversion ability (29.7 %). To take advantage of the abundant incoming –OH, nitration treatment is performed to obtain CDs-NO, which produces NO gas under the stimuli of glutathione (GSH) and hyperpyrexia. The consumption of GSH also benefits to the elevation of intracellular oxidative stress. Under NIR-II (1064 nm) irradiation, the generated ROS would combine with NO in-situ to form ONOO− radicals, which possess the higher cytotoxicity to cancer cells. The short distance between ROS and NO makes sure the quick conversion and high utilization of NO. The synergistic NO therapy and photo-thermal/dynamic therapy not only reveals remarkable therapeutic effect for tumor bearing mice but also arouses immune response for metastasis inhibition efficiently.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has become one of the most severe threats to human survive [1]. Compared with traditional cancer treatment methods (such as: chemotherapy, radiotherapy, surgical resection, etc.), phototherapy has received widespread attentions owing to the prominent advantages of non-invasiveness, spatial and temporal controllability, and low side effects [2]. Phototherapy, including: photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT), refers to the use of photosensitizers which can be stimulated by near infrared (NIR) light to introduce reactive oxygen species (ROS) or hyperpyrexia for inhibiting and ablating malignant tumors [3,4].

So far, metal (Au, Pt, Pd) [5] and semiconductor (FeS, CuS, TiOx) nanomaterials [6], and organic photosensitizers (porphyrin [7], hypocrellin [8], and phthalocyanine [9]) have been developed for phototherapy. The high metal ion concentration of metal photosensitizers and the low water solubility/photostability and easy aggregation of organic photosensitizers limit their practical applications. Therefore, the discovery of other nanomaterials is desired for phototherapy. Carbon-based nanomaterials with size below 10 nm are always called carbon dots (CDs), which are considered as core–shell materials. Their core mainly consists of graphitic carbon with the shell of amorphous carbon containing abundant functional groups, including: –COOH, –OH, and –NH2 [10–12]. With the wide absorption spectrum, tunable emission region, good water solubility, biocompatibility, bio-metabolism and photostability, CDs are widely used in sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery, and phototherapy as well [13,14]. For example, Sun et al. constructed an integrated multi-modality imaging and synergistic single laser-triggered PTT/PDT system by immobilizing the photosensitizer Ce6 on red-emitting carbon dots [15]. The combination of photosensitive heme and photothermal carbon dots revealed dual-mode phototherapy of cancer [16]. Zhao group synthesized CDs with lysosomal targeting by hydrothermal method using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene as raw material, which achieved PTT and PDT synergistic therapy under 635 nm laser irradiation [17].

Gas therapy (GT), including: NO, CO, H2S, H2, and SO2, is emerging as potential anti-cancer strategy [18–21]. Among these gases, nitric oxide (NO) is an important biological signaling molecule to regulate various physiological processes [22]. The low concentrations of NO promote cancer growth, ascribing to the enhanced angiogenesis and metastasis [23,24]. The higher levels of NO inhibit cancer progression by inducing apoptosis, sensitizing tumors to therapy means, reversing resistance, and retarding metastasis [25,26]. The development of NO donor is crucial for NO therapy to insure the delivery and release of NO to tumor region. Up to now, many kinds of NO donors, including S-nitrosoglutathiones [27], S-nitrosothiols [28], N-diazeniumdiolates [29], L-arginine [30] have been used to release NO under certain stimuli (such as: enzymes, pH, H2O2, light, and ultrasound). Moreover, NO combines with ROS to produce peroxynitrite anions (ONOO−) with great toxic that damage the DNA of tumor cells [31]. Therefore, the combination of NO donors and photosensitizers were constructed to enhance antitumous effect. Gong et al. designed a glutathione (GSH) sensitive NO generating mannana nanoparticles, which was combined with Ce6 to greatly improve the PDT efficacy [32]. Wan group constructed a porous coordination network nanoplatform containing NO donor L-arginine to achieve synergistic treatment with PDT and gas therapy [33]. ICG and L-arginine was loaded in to PLGA nanoparticle to reveal NO-enhanced PDT and the increased antitumor efficacy against highly malignant 4T1 tumors in mice [34]. However, the above strategies always require complex assembly and some segment without contribution for anticancer are unavoidable. Besides, NO is a gaseous radical with short half-life about 1 s [35], therefore the distance between NO and ROS should be considered to insure their quick combination to enhance the gas therapy.

Fig. 1B reveals nanodots of 5–10 nm in size and that is consistent with the hydrated ionic radius focusing at 8 nm as shown Fig. 1C. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image exhibits lattice fringe spacing of 0.34 nm, corresponding to the interplanar spacing of graphite carbon (002) plane. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern shows the peaks at 2θ = 25.8° and 43.7°, ascribing to (002) and (101) plane of graphite carbon (Fig. 1E). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) displays that the emerging bands at 1450, 1712, and 3330 cm−1 are ascribed to C-OH, C=O, and O–H of CDs. Compared with MB, the peak at 2938 cm−1 decreases, implying the oxidation of C-HX (Fig. 1F). The elemental composition is further analyzed by XPS. The survey XPS spectrum shows the characteristic peaks of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p (Fig. S1). C 1s XPS spectrum reveals the peaks of CC/CC, CO/CN, CO with combining energy at 284.7, 286.5, 288.5 eV (Fig. 1H). From Fig. 1I, besides the signal of C-N (399.3 eV) and NH (401.4 eV), the binding band of NO at 404.4 eV also can be found. And O 1s spectrum reveals peaks at 532.9 and 531.6 eV, corresponding to CO and CO (Fig. 1J). As displayed in Fig. 1K, the binding energies at 163.7 and 167.7 eV are attributed to SC and SOx band, respectively. Without the addition of H2SO4, no carbon material can be obtained and it also remains the character of MB (Fig. S2). Here, H2SO4 oxidizes MB molecules to induce some functional groups (COH, COOH, SOx, NO) which are served as active sites to make sure the following polymerization and dehydration (carbonization). As expected, D-band (1370 cm−1) and G-band (1572 cm−1) of graphite carbon are observed from Raman spectrum with relative intensity ratio of 0.73 (ID/IG), indicating certain graphite degree of as-synthesized CDs (Fig. 1G). Based on the above investigation, H2SO4 plays vital role in the process where it is regard as oxidization and dehydration (carbonization) agent to insure the preparation of carbon nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal and ROS generation of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of sample is investigated. From Fig. 2 A, the typical adsorption of MB at 664 nm disappears. CDs possess the great adsorption in whole spectrum as well as NIR-II region. The photothermal property is surveyed with laser irradiation (1064 nm, 0.8 W cm−2). From Fig. 2B, the temperature of CDs elevates fast as NIR-II light is put in. After irradiation for 10 min, temperature increases by 21.0 °C. While, MB solution just induces 5.5 °C increment owing to the negative harvest in NIR-II region. At the same time, it raises 3.5 °C for pure H2O. In view of above, the great NIR absorption endows CDs with high photothermal efficiency. In addition, temperature increment of CDs solutions is also dependent upon concentrations (Fig. 2C) that is further confirmed from the thermal imaging photos as present in Fig. 2D. Furthermore, photothermal conversion efficiency is measured and calculated to be 29.7 % (Fig. S3B). That is considerable with the previous reports of CNQD-CN (21.32 %) [37]. GQDs (33.45 %) [38], and Graphitic carbon nanocubes (40.4 %) [39]. In addition, after 5-cycle of illumination on–off, there is negligible attenuation of temperature increment, indicating the great thermal stability of CDs solution (Fig. S3E). The high photothermal conversion/stability and the controllable (concentration/intensity/time-dependent) performances make CDs be considered as potential PTT agent.

In addition, NIR-triggered ROS generation from CDs was evaluated by using DCFH-DA as indicator which is oxidized by ROS to form 2,7-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) with green fluorescence. Under 1064 nm-laser irradiation, the obvious emission peaks at about 520 nm testify the production of ROS. The stronger emission of CDs demonstrates the greater ROS generation than that of MB as shown in Fig. 2E. To distinguish the active species of ROS, singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) is used to discover the production of singlet oxygen (1O2) and hydroxyl radical (·OH), respectively (Fig. 2F-G). Furthermore, the specific ESR signals of 1O2 and ·OH is further confirmed by using 4-amino-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) and 5,5-dimethly-1-proline-N-oxide (DMPO) as indicators as shown in Fig. 2H. To investigate formation mechanism, AgNO3 is added to capture photogenerated electrons, decreasing ROS generation. The declined ROS production also is found when N2 is used to remove dissolved O2 as shown in Fig. 2I. On the contrary, methanol, typical hole scavenger, is added to increase ROS because it can induce electron accumulations. In view of the capture experiments, it is believed that O2 and photo-excited electrons to determine ROS generation and that is consistent with previous reports [40–42]. Moreover, intracellular ROS detection also was carried out with DCFH as dye and 4T1 as tumor cell model. In comparison with control, NIR, and CDs@PEG group, the obvious green fluorescence of CDs@PEG + NIR group confirms intracellular ROS generation as present in Fig. 2J and S9B. In view of Fig. 2, MB can be employed as raw material to synthesize carbon nanomaterial as potential PTT and PDT agent. Besides, glucose also is used as alternative source to prepare carbon nanomaterial according to the same strategy. As shown in Fig. S4B-C, as-synthesized CDs reveal the greater performance on photothermal conversion as well as ROS generation than CDs-Glucose. It is believed that the aromatic structure of MB benefits to graphite degree, testifying from the loser distance between graphite layers as present in XRD data (Fig. S4A). In addition, some heteroatoms (N and S) are always considered as the active site of carbon materials for charge transfer that is also proved by the smaller radius in EIS curve and higher transient photocurrent as displayed in Fig. S4D-E [43–45]. Based on the above investigation, the aromatic structure and heteroatoms of carbon source is significant to the great performance of as-synthesized CDs.

On account of the abundant –OH of CDs, the nitration reaction is carried out to synthesize –ONO2 modified carbon nanodots (C-NO) as NO donor (Fig. S5). FT-IR spectrum reveals the new band at 1731 and 1040 cm−1, attributing to –NO2 asymmetric stretch and ONO2 symmetrical stretching vibration of C-NO (Fig. 3 A). After nitration, N signal in XPS analysis increases obviously (Fig. S1) and the proportion of N-O increases from 20.93 to 33.79 % with the decrease of C-O from 13.77 to 7.49 %, indicating the successful nitration of CDs (Fig. S6). Fortunately, there is not obvious attenuation on photothermal and ROS generation of C-NO after nitration (Fig. S7). It is known that the organic nitrates could evolve NO under treatment by GSH [46–48]. And the GSH sensitive NO-generation is quantificationally analyzed by using typical Griess reagent [49,50]. As depicted in Fig. 3B, when C-NO sample is incubated with GSH, it takes 1 h to induce 31.26 μM of NO release, that further increase to 39.04 μM within 3 h. Without GSH, there is not obvious NO release. ESR spectrum also reveals significant NO radical signal by using 2-phenyl-4,4,5,5,-tetramethylimidazoline-1-oxyl-3-oxide (PTIO) as indicator (Fig. 3C). Moreover, the decreased N element and N-O proportion from XPS analysis and the weakened –NO2 and –ONO2 band in FT-IR spectra further verify NO release (Fig. 3A and S6). To evaluate intracellular NO generation, 4-amino-5-methylamino-2,7-difluorofluorescein (DAF-FM) is adopted as probe, which produces green fluorescence in the presence of NO. From Fig. 3D, the intracellular green fluorescence of C-NO@PEG group confirms NO production derived from endogenous GSH triggered NO release. Diethyl maleate (DEM), a typical shielding agent of GSH, is added to reveal noteworthy fluorescence attenuation that also is proved by Flow cytometry statistical analysis (Fig. S9A). These results demonstrate that NO production is dominated by the reduction of C-NO and NO is the product of nitrate reduction [51]. Interestingly, the higher cellular NO level is found under NIR irradiation, attributing to the hyperpyrexia derived from photothermal effect accelerating NO release that is also consistent with Fig. 3B. It is known that NO could combine with ROS to produce ONOO− with higher cytotoxicity [52]. The qualitative detection of ONOO− is investigated by using dihydrorhodamine 123 (DHR) as probe, which can react with ONOO− to produce green fluorescence [53,54]. From Fig. 3E, the combination of GSH and NIR irradiation (ROS) makes C-NO@PEG to induce ONOO−. However, the single GSH can not make the generation of ONOO− because the lack of ROS. Besides, the ONOO− generation is further verified by UV–Vis absorption spectrum based on the characteristic absorption peak at 302 nm of C-NO@PEG + GSH + NIR (Fig. S8A) [55]. To further demonstrate the advantage of the in situ NO generation and conversion, other NO donor (L-arginine, which can induce NO release triggered by ROS) is adopted to construct the ex-situ system that just make the lower ONOO− generation of L-Arg + CDs@PEG + NIR (Fig. S8B). On account of the above analysis, the in situ combination of NO and ROS (1O2 and ·OH) facilitates ONOO− generation. In addition, DCFH is used to discover the total ROS (1O2, ·OH, and ONOO−) production. From Fig. 3F, DCF fluorescence of C-NO@PEG + GSH + NIR is higher than that of C-NO@PEG + NIR owing to the great oxidative activity of ONOO− radical possesses [56,57]. As shown in Fig. 3H, C-NO@PEG + NIR reveals more intracellular green fluorescence than CDs@PEG + NIR owing to the generated ONOO−. When extra GSH is put in, the greater fluorescence is ascribed to more NO supplement and ONOO− production (Fig. S9C). Here, 5,5′-Dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) is adopted as GSH indicator, validating GSH depletion after treatment with CD-NO as shown in Fig. 3G. In addition, GSH a typical endogenous reductant, whose consumption is significant to disturb the intracellular redox homeostasis, boosting the oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced cytotoxicity of C-NO@PEG in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: PEG was adopted to improve the dispersion stability and the cell adhesion. From Fig. S10, C-NO@PEG can be dispersed in various media (water, PBS, DMEM and FBS). After 2 days of storage, it also remains favorable dispersibility (Fig. S11–S12). And then, FITC fluorescent dye is grafted to trace the uptake of C-NO@PEG by 4T1 cells. As revealed in Fig. S13, after incubation for 0.5 h, some green fluorescence can be found in cytoplasm. Prolonging incubation time, green fluorescence gradually increases. The fast uptake is owing to the small particle size and PEG modification. Under NIR-II irradiation, the high green fluorescence indicates that the cell phagocytosis can be promoted by hyperpyrexia from photothermal effect. To visualize the cytotoxicity derived from C-NO@PEG + NIR, AM and PI dye were used to stain for live and dead cells, respectively. 4T1 cells are co-cultured with C-NO@PEG of different concentrations for 12 h, and then they are irradiated (1064 nm) with different power densities for 20 min. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with the increase of C-NO@PEG concentrations or power intensities, green fluorescence weakens with red fluorescence enhancing significantly, indicating the adjustable cytotoxicity derived from the synergistic therapy. To quantitatively evaluate the synergistic therapeutic effect of C-NO@PEG, MTT test is carried out. From Fig. 4B, under dark condition C-NO@PEG displays good biocompatibility for both cancer and normal cells (Fig. S14). Even at high sample concentration of 250 μg mL−1, the cell survival rate still remains 97.9 ± 1.4 % (4T1) and 99.2 ± 1.2 % (L929) at 24 h. Moreover, the addition of GSH decreases cell viability to 73.4 ± 2.1 % (100 μg mL−1) owing to more NO release as shown in Fig. 4C. Under NIR irradiation, the cell survival rate of C-NO@PEG + NIR and C-NO@PEG + GSH + NIR further drop to 15.4 ± 1.6 % and 9.6 ± 1.0 %. However, CD@PEG + NIR reveals the higher cell viability (44.2 ± 4.2 %) by the fact that without NO supply there is not highly toxic ONOO− generation (Fig. 4D). In view of the above, it is the combination of PDT, PTT and GT to make sure the great cytotoxicity. Mitochondrial depolarization is related to early cell apoptosis. Here, JC-1 is selected as detection reagent to monitor mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP). As shown in Fig. 4E, the control, NIR, and pure C-NO@PEG group reveal obvious red fluorescence that is attributing to JC aggregate for normal MMP. The obvious green fluorescence of C-NO@PEG + NIR group is derived from JC monomer, indicating the damaged mitochondria with decreased MMP. Therefore, it can be proved that the synergistic treatment (PDT/PTT/GT) of C-NO@PEG + NIR induce mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to apoptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging and synergistic therapy (PDT/PTT/GT) in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Aroused by novel photothermal performance, the temperature of tumor is recorded at every 2 h with NIR-II irradiation for 10 min after intravenous injection of C-NO@PEG. As shown in Fig. S15, the temperature increment of tumor reaches the maximum after the injection for 6 h. It is believed that it takes 6 h for the injected C-NO@PEG to gather into tumor region mostly. And the following irradiation treatment is carried out after the injection for 6 h. From Fig. 5 A, infrared thermal imaging behavior is investigated in vivo. C-NO@PEG + NIR group reveals the obvious lightened-tumor region in comparison with control group. And the corresponding temperature also is recorded as shown in Fig. 5B, after irradiation for 10 min, the local tumor temperature raises by 19.7 °C, while that of control group is just 4.6 °C. The thermal imaging ability makes the precise diagnose and guide for anticancer treatment.

In view of the cytotoxicity in vitro, the use of C-NO@PEG in treating tumor-bearing mice in vivo is explored. 20 of mice were divided into 4 groups: control, NIR, C-NO@PEG and C-NO@PEG + NIR. The tumor volumes and body weights were monitored every 2 days. From Fig. 5C, every group reveals the slight increase of body weights and the physical and mental conditions of all mice are normal during treatment, implying the ignorable side-effect for both C-NO@PEG and NIR. After treatment for 14 days the tumor volume of control group gradually increases to 5.7 times as primary, while that of C-NO@PEG + NIR group closes to zero (Fig. 5D). And the corresponding photos further show the disappeared tumor of C-NO@PEG + NIR group, indicating that the synergistic PDT/PTT/GT inhibits tumor growth effectively. As shown in Fig. 5E, the sections of tumor are H&E staining, showing the remarkable cell shrinkage and tissue necrosis from C-NO@PEG + NIR group. In addition, the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) are also analyzed as shown in Fig. 5F. Their H&E staining slices display the negligible inflammatory lesions or tissue injury for the main organs. Moreover, all indications of routine blood analyses fall into the normal region in comparison with control group, demonstrating the favorable biocompatibility of both NIR and C-NO@PEG (Fig. S16).

It is well known that NO also can be regarded as vasodilator to relax blood vessels to improve the blood perfusion and the oxygen supply [58]. To further testify the vasodilatation ability of C-NO@PEG, the immunofluorescence staining assay with vascular endothelial cell marker CD31 was carried out for tumor slices. The more red fluorescence demonstrates the remarkable increased vascular endothelial cell for C-NO@PEG group (Fig. 6 B). Moreover, the decreased intratumoral HIF-1α level further confirms the amelioration of hypoxia as shown in Fig. 6C. Therefore, the improved blood perfusion and oxygen supply also could facilitate PDT to increase cytotoxicity. It is known that immunogenic cell death (ICD) could invoke an orchestrated anticancer immune response [58], and it is necessary to investigate whether the above synergistic treatments would arouse immunotherapy. ICD could provide adjuvant stimuli to promote maturation of dendritic cells (DCs), increasing the number of infiltrating immune cells, enhancing immunotherapy, and inhibiting tumor metastasis [59,60]. Firstly, cells were collected from inguinal lymph nodes of mice after treatment for 3 days and their co-stimulatory molecules (CD80 and CD86), which were regarded as the markers of DC maturation, are detected by flow cytometer (FCM). As shown in Fig. 6D, the percentage of DC maturation, reflected in the expression of CD80 and CD86, increases to 24.9 % for C-NO@PEG + NIR group that is higher than the other groups. In addition, C-NO@PEG + NIR group also reveals the greatest up-regulation of CD11c (DC marker) (Fig. 6E). The mature DCs would further express certain histocompatibility complex peptide to activate T cells and to produce various cytokines, such as TNF-α (critical cellular immunity mediator) [61], IFN-γ (primary production of some immune cells: CD8+ cytotoxic T and Th1 cells etc.) [62], and IL-2 (immune regulatory lymphokine) [63], to regulate immunity [64,65]. Then, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) was used to determine the levels of these cytokines in the serum of mice after different treatments. As shown in Fig. 6F-H, C-NO@PEG + NIR reveals the highest levels of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 that is 4.7, 4.5, and 2 times in comparing with control group, demonstrating that synergistic PTT/PDT/GT could effectively stimulate the immune response and reverse tumor immunosuppressive microenvironment. After the treatment, mice were further cultivated beyond 80 days, revealing the negligible tumor recurrence of C-NO@PEG + NIR group as shown in Fig. S17. Besides, the photos of lung and the corresponding H&E staining sections further reveal the lung metastatic nodules of control, NIR, and C-NO@PEG rather than C-NO@PEG + NIR group (Fig. 6I and S18). Based on the above investigation, the synergistic therapy can stimulate immune response to inhibit metastasis efficiently [66,67].

In this paper, the nitrated CDs were prepared with synergistic NO therapy, PTT, and PDT ability for anticancer. MB was used as carbon source via hydrothermal process with H2SO4 as oxidization and dehydration (carbonization) agent to make sure carbon nanomaterial production. The advantages of C-NO are listed as follows: (1) It reveals great adsorption in NIR-II region as well as the high photothermal conversion ability (PTT) and great ROS generation (PDT), ascribing to the aromas character and present heteroatom of MB. (2) The hydrothermal strategy introduces plenty of –OH on the surface of CDs, providing active site for nitration. As-synthesized nitrated CDs nanoparticles reveal NO release triggered by GSH and hyperpyrexia derived from photothermal. The consumption of GSH also benefits to the elevation of intracellular oxidative stress. (3) The released NO can be trapped in-situ by ROS to convert ONOO− radicals with higher cytotoxicity. The supplementary NO helps angiectasis to ameliorate tumor hypoxia to facilitate PDT. (4) Each component participates in the anticancer directly, endowing CDs-NO with great utilization and efficiency for anticancer. The synergistic therapy (PTT/PDT/GT) also efficiently arouses immune response for metastasis inhibition.
